item.title: Can UK house prices survive another economic shock?
item.description: The housing market looks to have made it through the crises of the 2020s unscathed — but they have weakened it significantly

item.title: An all too special relationship
item.description: Maga leaders obsess over an imagined version of a long-lost Britain

item.title: The UK’s unpleasant energy exposure
item.description: The vulnerability stems from history and geography rather than failed policies

item.description: What can investors do?

item.title: Car park collapse is a warning to leaseback-loving retailers
item.description: UK supermarkets that sell and then rent back their property should take note of NCP’s failure

item.title: Trump’s protectionist Nixon redux takes a darker turn than the original
item.description: Tricky Dick’s trade and currency war gave way to a pivot towards the multilateral

item.title: UK bank capital rules are becoming a drag on growth
item.description: Reforming the capital regime would unlock meaningful lending capacity and strengthen Britain’s competitiveness

item.title: How did I end up here? — Nigella Lawson’s first FT column
item.description: A defence of food writing (and a recipe for rice pudding) as Nigella returns to her alma mater newspaper

item.title: Why the Vatican is at war with Maga
item.description: Trump and Peter Thiel have managed to unite the disparate factions of the Catholic Church against an earthly enemy

item.title: Will $100 oil speed up the EV shift?
item.description: As fuel prices rise and the outlook becomes more uncertain, the economics of car choices and manufacturing become harder to ignore

item.title: Labour must get out of its own way
item.description: The government’s approach to policymaking has been self-defeating

item.title: Snap’s activist captures the future of shareholder rights
item.description: Tech group’s traded shares have zero votes, leaving shareholders without a voice

item.title: Shutting Hormuz is a template for China in Taiwan
item.description: Beijing will seek to replicate Tehran’s playbook in the Taiwan Strait — and the global economic impact could be even worse

item.title: How Mexico’s art world is fighting to keep Frida Kahlo
item.description: Santander’s plan to move the Gelman art collection to Spain has resulted in local anger

item.title: Mooted Pernod-Jack Daniel’s merger should age well
item.description: Given their fall in shares and a general decline in drinking, a get-together makes sense

item.title: Does it really make sense to retrain as a plumber?
item.description: It’s become the go-to advice for anyone whose white-collar job could be eliminated by AI — but it’s not very realistic

item.title: The Fed is vacillating as the war continues
item.description: This is starting to look like a central bank appeasing its political masters

item.title: Trump learns that not everyone has a price
item.description: The great cynic did not expect Iran to fight out of conviction

item.title: US investors prefer Europemaxxing to Europebashing
item.description: Venture capital firms know their dollars go a lot further here than in Silicon Valley

item.title: Our euro, your solution
item.description: Reducing the world’s dependency on the dollar is in the interest of every country that buys energy, issues debt or trades

item.title: Political drama reaches heart of UK’s nuclear deterrent
item.description: Captain steps back after his link to Chinese spy case triggers Royal Navy probe

item.title: The disappointing dollar

item.title: High street banks could learn from Revolut’s fee bonanza
item.description: Higher account fees would reduce exposure to interest rates for old-school lenders

item.title: The global economy turns out to be more resilient than we had feared
item.description: Trump’s tariffs have not led to the kind of retaliatory action many expected

item.title: Silent cells could mean no more scars
item.description: Scientists believe the key to improving skin regeneration lies in dialling down excessive nerve growth
